pattern: Water level at any cell is set by the lowest rim that surrounds it, so flooding inward from the border with a min-heap fixes each cell's height the first time it is reached.
hints: Water can only be held where every escape path to the outside passes over a higher wall, so the boundary cells start the analysis.
Process cells from lowest current water level outward: a min-heap of border cells lets you always expand the weakest part of the rim first.

When you pop the lowest rim cell and visit a neighbour, the neighbour's water level is the max of its own height and the rim level; the trapped amount is that level minus the cell height, and the neighbour re-enters the heap at its water level.

edge_inputs.description: small map with an interior dip
single deep central cell surrounded by a wall
a 1xN strip can hold no water
a notch in the rim lets all water drain out
the basin level is capped by the lowest surrounding wall, not the highest
two separate basins of different depths

stress.description: random heights with a guaranteed high border so basins form
stress.note: tall border maximises trapped water and exercises the heap ordering
